content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
January 11,
1994
Honorable Carolyn Nash
Councilmember
City of San Juan Capistrano
32906 Avenida Descanso
San Juan Capistrano,
CA
92675
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-481
Dear Councilmember Nash:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”) •1
Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any
conduct which may have already taken place.
In addition, this
letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The Commission does
not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
Finally, please note that other laws may apply to your
situation,
such as Government Code Section 1090.
Government Code
Section 1090 is not part of the Political Reform Act.
The
Commission’s jurisdiction is limited to the provisions of the Act.
You may wish to contact the Attorney General’s Office with respect
to this section,
and other provisions of law that might apply to
your facts.
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Sections,
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A—93—481
Page 2
OUESTIONS
1.
May you participate in decisions of the City of San Juan
Capistrano regarding the Historic Towne Center despite the fact
that your spouse’s employer, the San Juan Capistrano Research
Institute, operates on property leased from the city which is
adjacent to the Historic Towne Center project area?
2.
Do you have reporting obligations with respect to the
lease arrangement between the city and your spouse’ s employer, the
San Juan Capistrano Research Institute?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
You may participate in the decisions so long as the
decisions involving the Historic Towne Center will g:
(1) result
in an increase or decrease of gross annual receipts for a fiscal
year, or the value of the assets or liabilities of the San Juan
Capistrano Research Institute,
by $10,000 or more; or
(2) affect
the San Juan Capistrano Research Institute’s expenses by $2,500 or
more for a fiscal year.
2.
You have no disclosure responsibilities with respect to
the lease arrangement between the city and your spouse’s employer,
the San Juan Capistrano Research Institute.
FACTS
You are a member of the San Juan Capistrano City Council and
a member of the San Juan Capistrano Community Redevelopment
Agency.
The San Juan Capistrano Community Redevelopment Agency
will soon be considering a proposed development project, the
Historic Towne Center (the “project
’).
The city is currently
holding public workshops to determine what development and how
much development should occur on the project site.
Following the
workshops, the plan will be processed in a normal manner through
the city’s review boards and commissions.
Immediately adjacent to the project is a nonprofit entity,
the San Juan Capistrano Research Institute (the “institute”), for
which your spouse is the director of research.
The institute is a
charitable organization, incorporated as a public benefit
California corporation and is tax exempt pursuant to Internal
Revenue Code Section 501(c) (3),
and governed by a board of
trustees.
You stated that the institute’s funding for salaries and
operating. costs is derived entirely from grants awarded to the
institute by the National Aeronautics and Space Administration in
Washington,
D.C.
You are not an employee or officer, nor have you
ever been an employee or officer.
You receive no income from the
institute, but have performed volunteer services for the institute
on occasion.


Our File No.
A—93—48l
Page
3
You have also asked about your filing obligations with
respect to the institute’s rent—free occupancy of their premises.
You stated that the institute has leased the building from the
city free of charge for three years,
on the condition ‘that the
facility be available for meetings of other community
organizations.
You also stated that although the agreement
between the city and the institute did not require rent payments,
the institute voluntarily began paying $1,000 a month during 1991
and $2,000 a month beginning July 2,
1992, through February 11,
1993.
The City of San Juan Capistrano pays the building’s owner
approximately $7,000 a month for use of the building.
On April 6,
1993, the city council (without your
participation)
entered into a one—year lease with the owner of the
building and a one-year sublease with the institute, during which
time the full lease rent payment is made by the institute directly
to the owner of the building.
This relationship between the city
and the institute will terminate on February 14,
1994,
when the
institute will be the full tenant of the building on a lease with
the owner.
ANALYSIS
Economic Interests
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
As a San Juan Capistrano
City Councilmember, you are a public official under the Act.
(Section 82048; Regulation 18700)
Section 87103 provides:
An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:
***
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,


Our File No.
A—93—481
Page 4
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
***
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10—percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103(b)
and
(C).
1.
The Institute
Section .82030 provides that the income of an individual also
includes any community property interest in the income of a
spouse.
Consequently, if any person or business has been a source
of income to you of $250 or more, or to your spouse of $500 or
more
(making your community property interest $250) within the
past 12 months, the source of income is a potentially
disqualifying economic interest as described in Section 87103.2
Your spouse is employed by the San Juan Capistrano Research
Institute.
Presumably, your spouse has received more than $500
from the Institute.
Thus, the Institute is a source of income to
you and you may not participate in any governmental decision which
will have a foreseeable
3 and material financial effect on the
Institute.
2
However,
income paid to your spouse as separate property
income, pursuant to a bona fide separate property agreement, does
not create an economic .interest for you in the sources of the
income since you do not have a community property interest in the
income..
(Vassev Advice Letter,
No.
A—86—201.)
However, you have
not provided facts to indicate that a separate property agreement
exists.
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular decision.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(In
re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Our File No.
A-93--481
Page 5
2.
Real Property
Real property owned or leased by a public official is also
considered an economic interest.
You are also considered to have
an indirect interest in any. property owned by your immediate
family, or by any business entity or trust in which you or your
immediate family own 10—percent or more.
You have not described any ownership interest or leasehold
interest in real property.
You did state that the Institute
leases property adjacent to the property that is the subject of
the decision in question.
However, since,
among other reasons,
the institute is not a “business entity” under the Act,
4 you are
not presumed to have an interest in that lease.
Thus, while the
location of the property will be relevant in determining whether
the institute will be materially affected (as discussed below), it
would not result in you having an economic interest in the real
property or lease in question.
Materiality
The Commission has adopted differing guidelines to determine
whether the effect of a decision is material,
based on the
specific circumstances of each decision.
Regulation 18702.1(a)
provides that where a source of income is directly before your
agency as an applicant or the subject of the decision, the effect
of the decision is deemed to be material and disqualification is
required.
A source of income is directly before an agency when the
source initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim,
appeal,
or similar request, or is a named party in,
or the sublect
, the proceeding.
A source of income is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit,
or other entitlement
to,
or contract with, the subject person or business entity.
5
“Business entity” means any organization or enterprise operated
for profit,
including but not limited to a proprietorship,
partnership, firm, business trust,
joint venture,
syndicate,
corporation or association.
You are also required to disqualify yourself where the
Institute will be indirectly affected by a decision.
Regulation
18702.5
(copy enclosed)
provides differing dollar thresholds of
materiality depending on the financial size of the nonprofit
entity.
For example, for an entity whose gross annual receipts
are $100,000 or less, the effect of the decision will be material
if the decision will affect the entity’s gross annual receipts for
a fiscal year, or the value of the entity’s assets or liabilities,
by $10,000 or more; or the decision will affect the entity’s
expenses by $2,500 or more in a fiscal year.
(Regulation
18702.5(f).)


Our File No.
A-93--481
Page
6
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)
You have not described decisions
directly affecting the Institute
(such as the decision to grant
the lease of the property to the Institute).
However, the Act also requires that public officials
disqualify themselves from governmental decisions in which a
source of income is indirectly involved if the decisions will
materially affect the source of income.
(See e.g., Regulations
18702.2 and 18702.6.)
With respect to nonprofit entities,
Regulation 18702.5 provides different thresholds of materiality
which apply depending on the financial size of the entity.
For example, if the Institute’s gross annual receipts are
$100,000 or less, the effect of the decision will be material if
any of the following apply:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease of the entity’s gross annual receipts
for a fiscal year in the amount of $10,000 or more.
(2)
The decision will cause the entity to
incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or
eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in
the amount of $2,500 or more.
(3)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the entity’s assets or
liabilities in the amount of $10,000 or more.
Thus,
so long as the decision involving the Historic Towne
Center will not:
(1) result in an increase or decrease of the
Institute’s gross annual receipts for a fiscal year,
or the value
of the Institute’s assets or liabilities by $10,000 or more; or
(2) affect the Institute’s expenses for a fiscal year by $2,500 or
more,
you may participate in the decision.
You stated that the institute’s funding is derived entirely
from grants awarded to the institute by the National Aeronautics
and Spaóe Administration in Washington,
D.C.
Thus, it seems
unlikely that the project will have an affect on the institute.
However,
as stated above, the Commission does not act as the
finder of fact in providing advice.
Thus,
we must leave that
factual determination to you.


Our File No.
A-93—481
Page 7
Disclosure of Economic Interests
As a San Juan Capistrano City Counciliuember you are required
to disclose your investments and real property interests in your
jurisdiction,
and sources of income (including community property
income)
in the jurisdiction.
(Section 87203.)
In addition,
you
are required to disclose your pro—rata share of investments in,
real property held by, or income to any business entity that you
or your immediate family own 10 percent or more.
A “business entity” is defined as any organization or
enterprise operated for profit,
including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture,
syndicate, corporation or association.
While your spouse’s income
from the nonprofit institute would be reportable, assets of and
income to the nonprofit would not be reportable.
Thus, the lease
between the city and the institute creates no disclosure
responsibilities for you.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322_5901.6
Sincerely,
•Steven G.
Churchwell
il Counsel
John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
6
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0 per page.

adjacent to the Historic Towne Center project area?
2.

lease arrangement between the city and your spouse’ s employer, the
San Juan Capistrano Research Institute?

by $10,000 or more; or

the San Juan Capistrano Research Institute’s expenses by $2,500 or
more for a fiscal year.
2.

the lease arrangement between the city and your spouse’s employer,
the San Juan Capistrano Research Institute.

QUESTION: 1.
May you participate in decisions of the City of San Juan
Capistrano regarding the Historic Towne Center despite the fact
that your spouse’s employer, the San Juan Capistrano Research
Institute, operates on property leased from the city which is
adjacent to the Historic Towne Center project area?
2.
Do you have reporting obligations with respect to the
lease arrangement between the city and your spouse’ s employer, the
San Juan Capistrano Research Institute?

CONCLUSION: 1.
You may participate in the decisions so long as the
decisions involving the Historic Towne Center will g:
(1) result
in an increase or decrease of gross annual receipts for a fiscal
year, or the value of the assets or liabilities of the San Juan
Capistrano Research Institute,
by $10,000 or more; or
(2) affect
the San Juan Capistrano Research Institute’s expenses by $2,500 or
more for a fiscal year.
2.
You have no disclosure responsibilities with respect to
the lease arrangement between the city and your spouse’s employer,
the San Juan Capistrano Research Institute.
